query: cardiac arrhythmias associated with terfenadine and antibiotic inhibitors of CYP3A4

retrieved_docs.title: Ivabradine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: CYP3A4 inhibitors: CYP3A4 metabolizes ivabradine. Administering CYP3A4 inhibitors increases ivabradine plasma concentrations, while CYP3A4 inducers reduce them. Increased ivabradine levels may potentiate bradycardia and conduction abnormalities. The co-administration of potent CYP3A4 inhibitors with ivabradine is contraindicated. Strong inhibitors include azole antifungals such as itraconazole, macrolide antibiotics such as telithromycin and clarithromycin, HIV protease inhibitors, and nefazodone. [36] Verapamil and diltiazem are also CYP3A4 inhibitors; they increase ivabradine levels and, hence, the risk of bradycardia. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular agents (ivabradine): bradycardia, conduction disturbances

retrieved_docs.title: Fexofenadine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the findings of these studies, some case reports have suggested a potential association between fexofenadine and QT-interval elongation and ventricular arrhythmia. Concurrent use of fexofenadine with hepatic CYP3A4 blockers, such as erythromycin and ketoconazole, may elevate blood concentration levels. In contrast, combined use with drugs such as rifampin and troglitazone can decrease concentration levels. These effects are likely attributed to interactions between these compounds and the P-gp transporter, which facilitates the removal of fexofenadine from the bloodstream. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Ivabradine -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Negative chronotropic drugs: A significant proportion of patients who are prescribed ivabradine also receive treatment with a β-blocker. When ivabradine is combined with other medications that decrease heart rate, including digoxin, amiodarone, or additional β-blockers, the probability of bradycardia increases. [34] Therefore, it is essential to monitor heart rate closely in patients who are administered ivabradine alongside these negative chronotropic agents. CYP3A4 inducers: CYP3A4 inducers, such as St. John's wort, rifampicin, barbiturates, and phenytoin, should be avoided during ivabradine therapy. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome -- Epidemiology -- QTc Prolongation and Increased Risk of Arrhythmia
retrieved_docs.content: The use of haloperidol with protease inhibitors (PI) or azithromycin with ZDV can lead to an increase in QTc and result in cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: Bronchodilators -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Beta-2 agonists may also reduce serum digoxin levels, potentially decreasing its therapeutic efficacy; monitoring of digoxin concentrations is recommended when clinically indicated. LABAs may exhibit elevated plasma concentrations and an increased risk of QT prolongation or cardiac arrhythmias when co-administered with potent CYP3A4 inhibitors, such as ketoconazole or ritonavir. Such combinations should be avoided. When used concurrently with other QT-prolonging agents (eg, macrolide antibiotics and antipsychotics), there is an additive risk of ventricular arrhythmias, and electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring may be necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Moxifloxacin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Moxifloxacin may increase the anticoagulant effect of drugs such as warfarin, potentially leading to an increased risk of bleeding. Close monitoring of blood clotting parameters may be necessary when these drugs are used together. Combining moxifloxacin with certain antiarrhythmic drugs, such as quinidine, procainamide, amiodarone, and sotalol, can increase the risk of QT interval prolongation and potentially lead to life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. [27] This combination should be avoided or used cautiously under close medical supervision.

retrieved_docs.title: Nirmatrelvir-Ritonavir -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, dronedarone, flecainide, propafenone, quinidine): cardiac arrhythmias

retrieved_docs.title: Rifampin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Rifampin is an inducer of P-glycoprotein and can reduce the serum concentration of direct oral anticoagulants (DOAC), including dabigatran, apixaban, and rivaroxaban. As a result, their combined usage warrants caution. [48] [49] [50] The European Heart Rhythm guidelines suggest that utilizing DOACs alongside rifampin necessitates vigilance and monitoring. [51] Rifampin reduces the plasma concentration and efficacy of metoprolol and propranolol. [52] [53] Rifampin functions as a UGT1A1 inducer, which can lead to diminished concentrations of cabotegravir and raltegravir. Hence, concurrent administration of these drugs should be avoided. [54] [55] Concurrent administration of rifampin with hepatotoxic drugs, such as halothane, should be avoided due to the increased risk of hepatotoxicity. [56]

retrieved_docs.content: Arrhythmias Cardiac arrest Acute lung injury Agitation, hallucinations Seizures Heart failure Liver dysfunction

retrieved_docs.title: Antifungal Ergosterol Synthesis Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- (a) Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Azoles can increase the toxicity of other drugs when combined with drugs that are also metabolized by the CYP enzymes. A summary of the drug interactions is given below. [16] Ketoconazole Ketoconazole exerts inhibitory effects on cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP3A4, increasing plasma concentrations of drugs such as cyclosporine A, clarithromycin, and telithromycin. Ketoconazole should not be administered with dofetilide, quinidine, pimozide, lurasidone, cisapride, methadone, disopyramide, dronedarone, and ranolazine due to the potential for QT prolongation. Ketoconazole's interference with the metabolism of these drugs heightens the risk of serious cardiac arrhythmias, specifically torsades de pointes, necessitating caution and avoidance of concurrent use. Fluconazole

retrieved_docs.title: QT Prolonging Drugs -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Medication interactions are another form of dangerous contraindication. Certain QT-prolonging medications are substrates of the cytochrome P450 (CYP450) system. If a patient is using a CYP450 inhibitor medication at the same time, there is a risk of significantly greater QT prolongation.

retrieved_docs.title: Itraconazole -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Assessing the patient for any signs of current fungal infections Reviewing the patient’s cardiac history. If the patient has any history of arrhythmias or heart failure, consult cardiology. Ordering liver enzymes to evaluate the liver’s function. [2] Consult with an infectious disease physician to ensure that itraconazole will provide adequate coverage for the patient. Consult with the pharmacist to ensure no drug-drug interactions exist between itraconazole and the patient’s current medication; in severe cases, an infectious disease specialty pharmacist can be a valuable addition to the interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: Antifungal Ergosterol Synthesis Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- (a) Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor, causing increased levels of tacrolimus, cyclosporine A, glipizide, and midazolam. An interaction between posaconazole and digoxin resulted in atrial fibrillation and deteriorated into polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, mediated via P-glycoprotein. [38] Isavuconazole: Acting as a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor, it elevates plasma levels when co-administered with cyclosporine A, tacrolimus, sirolimus, and mycophenolate mofetil; it requires dose modification and vigilant monitoring. Oteseconazole Oteseconazole is a BCRP inhibitor, impacting drug transport mechanisms. When co-administered with BCRP substrates such as rosuvastatin, there exists a potential for increased exposure to the substrate. This exposure raises the risk of adverse reactions associated with BCRP substrates, underscoring the importance of careful monitoring during concurrent administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Theophylline -- Contraindications -- Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with cardiovascular disease: Use cautiously in patients who have cardiac arrhythmias (excluding bradyarrhythmias), at it may exacerbate arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2856
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone, an antiarrhythmic medication, is contraindicated for use with saquinavir, an HIV protease inhibitor, because co‑administration can lead to harmful drug interactions; therefore, clinicians should avoid prescribing these two agents together to prevent adverse effects and ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1617
retrieved_docs.content: Gatifloxacin, a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is contraindicated for use in patients who are receiving sotalol, a class III antiarrhythmic agent, because the combination can increase the risk of serious cardiac events such as QT‑interval prolongation and arrhythmias. Consequently, clinicians should avoid prescribing gatifloxacin to individuals currently treated with sotalol and consider alternative antimicrobial therapies to ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digoxin is contraindicated, meaning that digoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is contraindicated in patients receiving nelfinavir mesylate because of a significant drug interaction. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, which can alter the metabolism of other medications that are substrates of these enzymes. The combination of its contraindication with nelfinavir mesylate and its inhibitory effects on CYP2C9 and CYP3A highlights the importance of thorough medication review and monitoring when amiodarone is prescribed.

retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class relationships and CYP3A4‑mediated contraindications are essential for safe prescribing and for preventing serious adverse events such as hypotension, excessive drug exposure, and statin‑related muscle toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1667
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A. By suppressing the activity of these metabolic pathways, amiodaron can alter the clearance of drugs that are substrates of CYP2C9 or CYP3A, increasing the risk of drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing amiodaron’s inhibitory effects on both CYP2C9 and CYP3A is essential for clinicians when selecting and dosing concomitant medications, to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2202
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone exhibits a mechanism of action that aligns with the pharmacologic profile of adrenergic beta‑antagonists, indicating that its therapeutic effects involve beta‑adrenergic blockade. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP3A, classifying it as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent. This combination of beta‑antagonist activity and CYP3A inhibition influences both its antiarrhythmic efficacy and its potential for drug‑drug interactions, making awareness of these properties essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 467
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) metabolizes mikonazol, amiodarone, and omeprazole, while amiodarone and mikonazol both inhibit CYP2C9 and CYP3A, creating potential drug‑drug interactions for agents that rely on these pathways. Amiodarone is an anti‑arrhythmic used in cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation but is contraindicated with ritonavir because ritonavir strongly inhibits CYP3A4, raising amiodarone levels and toxicity risk. Acebutolol, a beta‑blocker, is also capable of treating cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, whereas digoxin is contraindicated in these life‑threatening arrhythmias. The NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology list several agents relevant to COVID‑19 management, including aspirin, clindamycin, kalcifediol, omeprazole, ritonavir, zinc sulfate, and dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA, classified in the ATC system as an other sex hormone/modulator of the genital system). Aspirin frequently co‑occurs with ranitidine and digoxin, and digoxin also co‑occurs with ranitidine, reflecting common prescribing patterns for cardiovascular conditions and gastrointestinal protection. Collectively, these metabolic relationships, inhibitory effects, contraindications, therapeutic uses, and co‑prescription patterns underscore the necessity of careful medication management when drugs share CYP450 pathways or are employed in complex clinical scenarios such as COVID‑19 infection and acute cardiac emergencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is a potent inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, which can alter the metabolism of many concomitant medications. In addition to its enzyme‑inhibiting properties, amiodarone may be employed therapeutically to treat cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, conditions characterized by life‑threatening disturbances in heart rhythm. Bretylium tosylate, another antiarrhythmic agent, may be used to prevent the occurrence of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation. Understanding that amiodarone both interferes with CYP2C9 and CYP3A activity and can be used in the acute management of severe arrhythmias, while bretylium tosylate serves a prophylactic role, is essential for optimizing patient care and avoiding adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is identified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2C9, which can alter the metabolic clearance of drugs that are substrates of this pathway. Because of this inhibitory effect, amiodaron is contraindicated for use with saquinavir, a protease inhibitor that relies on CYP2C9 for its metabolism. The interaction between amiodaron’s CYP2C9 inhibition and saquinavir’s metabolic requirements can lead to elevated saquinavir levels and increased risk of toxicity. Clinicians should therefore avoid co‑prescribing amiodaron and saquinavir and consider alternative therapies or dose adjustments when managing patients who require either medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digitoxin is contraindicated; digitoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening arrhythmias because it can exacerbate cardiac instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: Tricyclic antidepressants are contraindicated in patients who are being treated with dronedarone hydrochloride, meaning that co‑administration of these antidepressant agents should be avoided due to the risk of adverse drug interactions. Dronedarone hydrochloride, a class III antiarrhythmic medication used for rhythm control in atrial fibrillation and related cardiac arrhythmias, may have harmful pharmacodynamic or pharmacokinetic interactions with tricyclic antidepressants, which are commonly prescribed for depressive disorders. Clinicians should therefore assess medication histories carefully and consider alternative therapies when both conditions are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 320
retrieved_docs.content: shared metabolic enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2C9), inhibitory effects, contraindications, and inclusion in disease‑specific terminologies, emphasizing the importance of integrated drug‑interaction knowledge for safe and effective patient care across infectious, oncologic, cardiovascular, and other therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is both a substrate and an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme system. Specifically, amiodaron is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, an enzyme that metabolizes a wide range of chemicals and drugs. At the same time, amiodaron is classified as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent, meaning it can suppress the activity of CYP3A enzymes. This dual role as a substrate of CYP3A4 and an inhibitor of CYP3A highlights the potential for amiodaron to affect the metabolism of other concurrently administered medications, making awareness of drug‑drug interactions essential in patient care.
